skill.name: meta-manuscript-generator
skill.description: Generates a first draft of a clinical meta-analysis paper. Input the research report (including Methods and Results sections), language, and title to automatically generate a complete paper draft including Abstract, Introduction, Discussion, and other sections, with automatic PubMed retrieval of relevant references. Suitable for assisting in the writing of systematic reviews and meta-analyses.
skill.summary: Generate a first draft of a clinical meta-analysis manuscript with PubMed-backed references, from your research report.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Static analyzer flagged 83 potential issues driven by pattern matching on markdown backticks (48 false positives for shell execution), 'PMID' substrings mistakenly matched as weak crypto hashes, and legitimate PubMed API URLs flagged as hardcoded. After evaluating every finding, all high-severity static detections are false positives. The skill's real risk is low: it executes Python scripts that make HTTP calls to the NIH PubMed E-utilities API (eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov), a well-known public biomedical API. No data exfiltration, no malicious network destinations, and no prompt injection attempts were found. The Python scripts use stdlib urllib with proper parameter encoding. All network activity serves the documented academic writing purpose.

low_findings.title: External Network Calls to PubMed API
low_findings.description: Python scripts make HTTP requests to the NIH PubMed E-utilities API (eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov) using urllib.request. URLs are hardcoded to this well-known NIH domain. User-provided keywords and PMIDs are used to construct API query parameters, properly encoded via urllib.parse.urlencode. No data is sent to non-NIH servers. This is legitimate behavior for an academic reference retrieval tool, but the network access is real and should be documented.

low_findings.title: Python Script Execution for Reference Processing
low_findings.description: SKILL.md instructs the AI assistant to execute Python scripts (scripts/insert_references.py, scripts/search_references.py) as part of the manuscript generation workflow. The scripts are part of the skill package and perform well-defined tasks: PubMed reference search and citation insertion. No shell command injection vectors exist; all script arguments are controlled by the skill workflow, not raw user input.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown Backticks Misidentified as Shell Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged 48 locations across SKILL.md and references/writing-guide-en.md as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution'. These are all markdown inline code formatting backticks (e.g., `[PMID: xxxxxxxx]`, `## Discussion`, `python scripts/insert_references.py`). No actual shell execution occurs in these markdown documentation files. All 48 findings are false positives.

dangerous_patterns.title: PMID Substrings Misidentified as Weak Cryptographic Hashes
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged 8 locations as 'Weak cryptographic algorithm'. These are false positives caused by pattern matching on the substring 'MD' within 'PMID' (PubMed Identifier) references, which are standard biomedical citation markers (e.g., PMID: 38887558). No cryptographic code exists anywhere in this academic writing skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Legitimate PubMed URLs Misidentified as Suspicious Hardcoded URLs
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged 7 hardcoded URLs. All point to well-known legitimate destinations: the NIH PubMed E-utilities API (eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov), PubMed article links (pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov), and the skill's GitHub source repository. These are expected URLs for an academic writing tool that retrieves biomedical references. No URLs point to suspicious, unknown, or data-exfiltration endpoints.

content.user_title: Generate Clinical Meta-Analysis Manuscripts with PubMed References
content.value_statement: Writing a meta-analysis manuscript from a research report is time-consuming and requires careful reference management. This skill automates the full drafting process by generating Abstract, Introduction, and Discussion sections while automatically retrieving and formatting PubMed references in AMA citation style.
actual_capabilities: Generates structured Abstract, Introduction, and Discussion sections from a research report containing Methods and Results
Retrieves relevant PubMed references using the NIH E-utilities API based on extracted keywords
Formats in-text citations as AMA-style numbered references with clickable PubMed links
Supports both English and Chinese language input for manuscript generation
Produces a complete manuscript draft integrating user-provided Methods/Results with AI-generated sections
Processes PMID markers through Python scripts for automated citation insertion and reference list generation
limitations: Requires user to provide complete Methods and Results sections as input (cannot generate these sections)
PubMed search is limited to publications from 2020-2025 and returns at most 35 references total
Output quality depends on the completeness and accuracy of the input research report
Generated manuscript is a first draft requiring human review for scientific accuracy before submission

use_cases.title: Clinical Researcher Writing a Systematic Review
use_cases.description: A clinical researcher has completed their meta-analysis and needs to draft a manuscript for journal submission. They provide their Methods and Results sections and receive a complete first draft with Abstract, Introduction, and Discussion, along with PubMed-verified references.

use_cases.title: Graduate Student Preparing a Thesis Chapter
use_cases.description: A graduate student has completed the statistical analysis for their meta-analysis thesis chapter. They use the skill to generate a properly formatted draft with relevant literature citations, saving hours of manual reference searching and formatting.
use_cases.target_user: Graduate and doctoral students in health sciences

use_cases.title: Academic Writing Service Preparing Multiple Manuscripts
use_cases.description: An academic writing consultant handles multiple meta-analysis projects and needs consistent, reproducible drafts. They use the skill to standardize output quality across different projects while maintaining proper citation formatting for each manuscript.
use_cases.target_user: Academic writing consultants and medical editors

prompt_templates.title: Basic Manuscript Generation
prompt_templates.prompt:
Generate a meta-analysis manuscript draft using the meta-manuscript-generator skill. Here is my research report:

Methods and Results:
[Paste your complete methods and results content here]

Language: English

Title: [Your paper title]
prompt_templates.scenario: You have a completed meta-analysis and want a full manuscript draft

prompt_templates.title: PubMed Reference Retrieval Only
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the meta-manuscript-generator skill to search PubMed for references related to my research topic. My research direction is: [describe your clinical research topic and key variables]. Search for references suitable for the Introduction and Discussion sections.
prompt_templates.scenario: You want to find relevant PubMed references before writing

prompt_templates.title: Discussion Section Only
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the meta-manuscript-generator skill to write only the Discussion section of my meta-analysis paper. Here are my key results and statistics: [paste results with OR, HR, CI, p-values]. Compare these findings with existing literature and discuss clinical implications and limitations.
prompt_templates.scenario: You have written the other sections and only need the Discussion

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Language Chinese Manuscript
prompt_templates.prompt: 使用meta-manuscript-generator技能生成一篇meta分析论文的初稿。语言：中文。标题：[您的论文标题]。以下是研究方法和结果：[粘贴完整的方法和结果内容]。请确保PubMed参考文献检索以英文关键词进行，但论文正文使用学术中文撰写。
prompt_templates.scenario: You need a manuscript draft in Chinese with English PubMed references

output_examples.input: Generate a meta-analysis manuscript with the title 'Efficacy of PD-1 Inhibitors in Advanced NSCLC: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis' using English language.
output: Background: PD-1 inhibitors have emerged as a standard treatment for advanced non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), but their efficacy varies across patient populations.
Methods: We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials...
Results: Across 12 studies including 5,847 patients, PD-1 inhibitors significantly improved overall survival (HR = 0.72, 95% CI: 0.65-0.80, p < 0.001)...
Conclusions: PD-1 inhibitors demonstrate consistent survival benefit in advanced NSCLC...
Lung cancer remains the leading cause of cancer-related mortality worldwide, with NSCLC accounting for approximately 85% of cases [[1]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38887558/)...
This meta-analysis of 12 RCTs confirms that PD-1 inhibitors significantly improve overall survival in advanced NSCLC [[3]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37654321/)...
[1] Smith AB, Chen Y, et al. Global cancer statistics 2024. CA Cancer J Clin. 2024;74(3):229-263. [https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38887558/]

output_examples.input: Search PubMed for references on 'immune checkpoint inhibitors non-small cell lung cancer efficacy meta-analysis' for the Introduction section.
output: === keywords: immune checkpoint inhibitors non-small cell lung cancer efficacy meta-analysis ===
PMID: 38887558 | Smith AB, Chen Y, et al. Global cancer statistics 2024. CA Cancer J Clin. 2024.
PMID: 37654321 | Johnson KL, Williams RT, et al. PD-1 inhibitors in advanced NSCLC. Lancet Oncol. 2024.
PMID: 36543210 | Brown ME, Davis PK, et al. Systematic review of immunotherapy biomarkers. J Clin Oncol. 2023.

best_practices: Provide complete Methods and Results sections with detailed statistics (OR, HR, CI, p-values) for the best generated Discussion quality
Review all generated PubMed references for relevance and accuracy before including them in the final manuscript
Treat the generated draft as a first version that requires domain expert review for scientific accuracy and clinical nuance
anti_patterns: Do not submit the generated manuscript without thorough human review of references, statistics, and clinical interpretations
Do not expect the skill to generate Methods or Results sections from scratch (it only processes what you provide)
Do not use for regulatory submissions or clinical guidelines without expert validation and independent literature verification

faq.question: What input does this skill require from me?
faq.answer: You need to provide a complete research report containing Methods and Results sections, the desired language (Chinese or English), and the paper title. The Methods and Results sections are inserted directly into the final manuscript.

faq.question: How does the PubMed reference retrieval work?
faq.answer: The skill extracts keywords from your research topic, builds PubMed search queries, and calls the official NIH E-utilities API to find recent publications (2020-2025). It ranks results by relevance and returns formatted citations with PMIDs.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for non-medical meta-analyses?
faq.answer: The skill is designed for clinical and biomedical meta-analyses that use PubMed-indexed literature. For non-medical fields, the PubMed reference retrieval may not find relevant literature, though the writing structure could still serve as a template.

faq.question: What citation format does the skill produce?
faq.answer: The skill generates AMA (American Medical Association) style citations. In-text citations appear as numbered links like [[1]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/PMID/), and the reference list includes authors, title, journal, year, volume, issue, and pages.

faq.question: How many references can the skill retrieve?
faq.answer: The skill retrieves up to 15 articles for the primary keyword, 10 for the secondary keyword, and 5 for each additional keyword. References are limited to publications from 2020-2025 and are sourced from PubMed only.

faq.question: Is the generated manuscript ready for journal submission?
faq.answer: No. The output is a first draft intended to accelerate your writing process. You should verify all references for accuracy, review clinical interpretations, adjust section word counts to target journal requirements, and ensure scientific accuracy before submission.
